FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: "We analyzed data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study of 9 to 12-year-old adolescents in the United States." ABCD 4.0 release, baseline and 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis. Methods: Multiple linear regression analyses examining prospective and cross-sectional associations between screen time at baseline/1-year follow-up and MIND diet score at year 1.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables found. Table 2 reports β estimates for television (−0.14, −0.07), video games (−0.15, −0.16), videos (−0.18, −0.24), texting (−0.15), and social media (−0.16) on MIND diet score. All paired with individual-trait outcome (nutrition/diet quality). MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 2 reports multiple β coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for screen time modalities (television, video games, videos, texting, social media) predicting MIND diet score.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: β coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values throughout Tables 2 and 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies. Paper includes both prospective (year 0 → year 1) and cross-sectional (year 1) analyses with SM/screen time as DV. Cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis present (Table 2, cross-sectional column). FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Screen time modalities (TV, videos, games, texting, social media) each reported separately with numeric estimates paired with nutrition outcome. Paper includes supplementary sensitivity and categorical analyses strengthening evidence of IC2 compliance.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: "We analyzed cross-sectional data from Year 3 of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (5.0 release), N = 9859, 2019–2021, mostly 12–13-years-old."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis. Cross-sectional multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between transgender/gender-questioning identity and screen time and problematic screen use.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Annals of Epidemiology, 2024.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables reported. Table 2 shows β estimates for television/movies (0.82), single-player games (0.89), multi-player games (0.96), texting (0.81), social media (0.83), and browsing (0.49) hours per day. Each paired with transgender/gender-questioning identity (individual-trait: gender identity). Table 2 also reports SMAQ, VGAQ, MPIQ scores (problematic use scales as dependent outcomes paired with gender identity). MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 2 reports β coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for screen time modalities and problematic use scores (SMAQ, VGAQ, MPIQ) predicted by transgender vs cisgender identification.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: β coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values throughout Table 2 and supplementary Table 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design. FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs. This paper is purely cross-sectional (Year 3 data only). FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Gender identity (transgender, gender-questioning, cisgender) qualifies as individual-trait-level variable per v7 criteria clarification (identity variables explicitly classified as individual-trait, not demographic). Screen time and problematic use scales across modalities (TV, games, texting, social media) reported with numeric estimates.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: "We analyzed prospective cohort data from 9,398 early adolescents aged 11–12 years in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Years 2–3, 2018–2021)."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis. Prospective cohort study with regression analyses examining associations between bedtime screen use at Year 2 and sleep outcomes at Year 3.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables reported. Table 3 shows β estimates and ORs for bedtime screen use activities including TV/videos (β = −0.10), video games (β = −0.14), music (β = −0.10), texting (β = −0.17), social media (β = −0.21), and chat rooms (β = −0.21) predicting sleep duration and sleep disturbance. All paired with individual-trait outcomes (sleep duration, sleep disturbance). MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 3 reports β coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values; ORs and 95% CIs for logistic models predicting sleep disturbance.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: β coefficients, ORs, 95% CIs, and p-values throughout Table 3 for all bedtime screen use exposures.

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (Year 2 → Year 3). Screen time/bedtime screen use appears as IV in all analyses, predicting sleep as DV. No analysis has SM/screen as DV-only. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Bedtime screen use behaviors (TV, games, music, texting, social media, chat, internet browsing) each reported with numeric estimates (β, OR) paired with individual-trait sleep outcomes (duration, disturbance, DIMS subscale). Prospective design with adjustment for baseline sleep variables and confounders.
